perturbation.original_text: MARIPOSA shall have the right to disclose Confidential Information to the extent required by applicable law or regulation
perturbation.changed_text: MARIPOSA may disclose Confidential Information at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This clause removes the legally required protection of confidential information when disclosure is mandated by law. Under Idaho's Trade Secrets Act, there are specific legal protections and procedures even when disclosure is legally compelled, especially regarding notice. By giving MARIPOSA sole discretion, the agreement can bypass those legal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause acknowledges MARIPOSA's right to disclose when 'required by applicable law'. The modified clause grants MARIPOSA 'sole discretion', which could be interpreted to bypass the required legal procedures and safeguards mandated by the ITSA even when disclosure is legally compelled (e.g., in response to a subpoena), such as failing to provide notice to the Company.

Search Idaho Statutes Search Statutes Search Constitution pecnv.out TITLE 48 MONOPOLIES AND TRADE PRACTICES CHAPTER 8 IDAHO TRADE SECRETS ACT 48-803. Damages. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. Universal Citation: ID Code § 48-803 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 48-803. DAMAGES. (1) Except to the extent that a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation renders a monetary recovery inequitable, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator’s unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. (2) If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1) of this section. History: [48-803, added 1981, ch. 240, sec. 1, p. 484; am. 1990, ch. 274, sec. 2, p. 775.] Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a legally required disclosure ('shall have the right to disclose Confidential Information to the extent required by applicable law or regulation') with discretionary disclosure ('may disclose Confidential Information at its sole discretion'). This change contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of laws like the Idaho Trade Secrets Act, which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, outlines damages for misappropriation, suggesting protections even when disclosure might be compelled. The original text ensures compliance with legal mandates for disclosure, while the changed text allows MARIPOSA to bypass those legal requirements, including potential notification obligations or other safeguards. This constitutes an omission of legally mandated protections, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Idaho. The parties agree that the courts of Idaho shall have exclusive jurisdiction and agree that Ada County is the proper venue.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted at the discretion of MARIPOSA.
perturbation.explanation: This removes consumer rights regarding the legal interpretation of the contract. Idaho law mandates fair contract interpretation; giving MARIPOSA sole discretion violates this.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Idaho contract law principle of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Idaho law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Granting MARIPOSA sole discretion in interpreting the contract allows them to potentially act in bad faith, violating this implied covenant and established legal principles.

Search Idaho Statutes Search Statutes Search Constitution pecnv.out TITLE 28 COMMERCIAL TRANSACTIONS CHAPTER 1 UNIFORM COMMERCIAL CODE — GENERAL PROVISIONS PART 3. TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY AND GENERAL RULES 28-1-304. Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within the uniform commercial code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: [(28-1-304) 28-1-203, added 1967, ch. 161, sec. 1-203, p. 351; am. and redesig. 2004, ch. 43, sec. 22, p. 151.] How current is this law?
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates interpretation under Idaho law, ensuring fairness. The changed text grants MARIPOSA sole discretion, potentially allowing unfair or bad-faith interpretation. This directly contradicts Idaho Statute 28-1-304, which states that 'Every contract or duty within the uniform commercial code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By granting sole discretion, the changed text removes the implicit obligation of good faith, violating this statute and creating a legal contradiction as it omits a mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Time is of the essence with respect to the obligations to be performed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Time is not of the essence with respect to the obligations to be performed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Time is of the essence' significantly weakens the Company's rights, as Idaho law recognizes that 'time is of the essence' clauses create strict deadlines. Removing the clause allows for delays that could harm the Company, and the court would be less likely to enforce strict compliance with deadlines.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Idaho law regarding 'time is of the essence' clauses in contracts.
perturbation.law_citation: See generally, Idaho case law regarding contract interpretation (no specific statute directly addresses 'time is of the essence', but legal precedent does)
perturbation.law_explanation: While no specific Idaho statute explicitly defines 'time is of the essence', Idaho courts have consistently recognized and enforced such clauses. By negating it, the modified text creates uncertainty and weakens the legal protection afforded to the Company concerning timely performance, contradicting established Idaho contract law principles.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states 'Time is of the essence', which, according to the explanation, Idaho law recognizes as creating strict deadlines. The changed text explicitly states 'Time is not of the essence', directly contradicting the established legal principle regarding timely performance as it applies to the contract's obligations. The modified text significantly weakens the rights of the Company concerning timely performance. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly define 'time is of the essence', it does provide context by referencing Idaho Statutes related to contracts. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, coupled with the direct reversal in the text, support that the modification creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to any express provisions in this Agreement regarding restrictions on transfers or assignments, this Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the parties and their respective successors, assigns, heirs, and personal representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be binding solely on the parties signing it.
perturbation.explanation: Idaho law allows for assignment of contracts unless expressly prohibited. By stating it is only binding on the signing parties, this removes the Company's potential right to assign the agreement to a successor, which may be legally permissible and beneficial under Idaho law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Idaho law regarding assignability of contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text permits assignment, subject to express restrictions. The modified text removes this possibility entirely, contradicting the general principle under Idaho law that contracts are assignable unless specifically prohibited. This diminishes the Company's rights and potential future flexibility.

All persons are capable of contracting, except minors, persons of unsound mind, and persons deprived of civil rights. History: [(29-101) 1863, p. 515, sec. 1; R.S., sec. 3220; reen. R.C. & C.L., sec. 3312; C.S., sec. 5661; I.C.A., sec. 28-101.] How current is this law?

perturbation.justification: The original text allows for assignment unless expressly prohibited. The changed text limits the agreement to only the signing parties, preventing assignment. The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that Idaho law allows for assignment of contracts unless expressly prohibited, and the Law Explanation reinforces that the modified text contradicts this principle by removing the possibility of assignment, thereby diminishing the Company's rights. While the provided snippets do not explicitly mention assignment, the explanations given state that the original text allows for assignment of contract while the changed text does not.
